prompt: I have a function that returns one of two different closures depending on a boolean flag. The compiler says the two closure types are incompatible even though they both take an i32 and return an i32.
error_message:
error[E0308]: `if` and `else` have incompatible types
 --> src/main.rs:5:9
  |
3 |       if add {
4 |           |x| x + 1
  |           ---------- expected because of this
5 |       } else {
6 |           |x| x - 1
  |           ^^^^^^^^^ expected closure, found a different closure
  |
  = note: no two closures, even if identical, have the same type
  = help: consider boxing your closure and/or using it as a trait object
explanation: Each closure literal in Rust has its own unique anonymous type, even if two closures have the same signature and body. `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` in return position means the function always returns the same concrete type — so both branches must be the same type, which they never are. The solution is `Box<dyn Fn(i32) -> i32>`: boxing erases the concrete type behind a trait object, so both branches can return different closure types unified under the same `Box<dyn Fn>`. The trade-off is a heap allocation and a virtual dispatch; if the function isn't on a hot path this is fine. Alternatively, if all call sites are known, consider returning an enum wrapping each variant.

prompt: I'm trying to return a closure from a function, but the closure captures a reference to a local variable. The compiler says the returned closure references data owned by the current function.
error_message:
error[E0515]: cannot return value referencing local variable `name`
 --> src/main.rs:2:5
  |
2 |     move || format!("Hello, {}!", name)
  |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  |     |                            ---- `name` is borrowed here
  |     returns a value referencing data owned by the current function
  |
  = note: closures cannot outlive their borrows of stack-allocated variables
explanation: A closure that captures `name: &str` borrows whatever that &str points to. The returned `impl Fn()` would then borrow data that lives outside the function — the caller's string — which is fine, but the compiler needs the lifetime written out. Adding `+ '_` to the return type (`impl Fn() -> String + '_`) tells the compiler that the closure's lifetime is tied to the `&str` input. Alternatively, accept an owned `String` and `move` it into the closure; the closure then owns its data and has no lifetime constraint, making it `'static`.

prompt: I want to return a closure that mutates a counter each time it's called. I declared the return type as `impl Fn() -> i32` but the compiler says the closure is FnMut, not Fn.
error_message:
error[E0525]: expected a closure that implements the `Fn` trait, but this closure only implements `FnMut`
 --> src/main.rs:3:5
  |
3 |     || {
  |     ^^
  |     |
  |     this closure implements `FnMut`, not `Fn`
4 |         count += 1;
  |         ----- closure is `FnMut` because it mutates the variable `count` here
  |
note: the return type of a function must have a statically known size
  = help: return type was specified here
explanation: A closure that mutates captured state implements FnMut (can be called multiple times but requires &mut self each time), not Fn (which requires only &self and must be safe to call concurrently). Changing the return type to `impl FnMut() -> i32` fixes the mismatch. The caller must also declare `counter` as `mut` because calling an FnMut closure is a mutable operation. Adding `move` ensures `count` is owned by the closure rather than borrowed, which matters when the closure outlives the current stack frame. The hierarchy is: Fn ⊂ FnMut ⊂ FnOnce — every Fn is an FnMut, but not vice versa.

prompt: I need to return a closure from a function and then send it to another thread. The closure captures an Rc<T> which is not Send, so I get an error about the closure not being Send.
error_message:
error[E0277]: `Rc<Vec<i32>>` cannot be sent between threads safely
 --> src/main.rs:13:19
  |
13|     thread::spawn(handler).join().unwrap();
  |     ------------- ^^^^^^^ `Rc<Vec<i32>>` cannot be sent between threads safely
  |     |
  |     required by a bound introduced by this call
  |
  = help: within `impl Fn()`, the trait `Send` is not implemented for `Rc<Vec<i32>>`
  = note: required because it captures `data: Rc<Vec<i32>>`
  = note: required by a bound in `thread::spawn`
explanation: A closure captures its environment by value (when `move` is used) or by reference. If any captured variable is not Send, the closure itself is not Send, preventing it from being passed to thread::spawn. Rc<T> is not Send because its reference count is not atomic — concurrent access from multiple threads would cause data races. The fix is Arc<T> (atomically reference-counted), which is Send + Sync when T: Send + Sync. Annotating the return type as `impl Fn() + Send` makes the Send requirement explicit at the API boundary, producing a clearer error if the closure ever captures a non-Send type in the future.

prompt: I want a factory that builds a one-shot callback returning FnOnce. The calling code tries to call the returned closure twice and I need it to be a compile error, but it compiles and panics instead.
error_message: thread 'main' panicked at 'closure already consumed!', src/main.rs:5:13
explanation: FnOnce closures consume their captures — calling them moves out owned values, so they can only be called once. FnMut requires only &mut self, so it can be called multiple times; you had to use a runtime flag to enforce the one-shot contract, which only catches the violation at runtime. Returning `Box<dyn FnOnce() -> String>` makes the contract compile-time: calling `cb()` moves `cb`, so any second call is a use-after-move error (E0382). The closure body `|| msg` moves the captured String out, which is exactly what FnOnce allows. The box is necessary because FnOnce trait objects aren't Sized, so they must be heap-allocated to be returned.
